funFact: A is the third most used letter in English and makes 3 distinct sounds.
E is the most common letter in the entire English language.
Short I is one of the trickiest sounds for non-native speakers.
O is the oldest letter — it comes from ancient Phoenician.
U always follows Q in English words — they are best friends.
Y is both a consonant AND a vowel — the only letter that is both.
B is silent in words like LAMB and CLIMB.
C says /k/ before A, O, U, or any consonant.
D is one of the most reliable consonants — it almost always says /d/.
F used to be written as a double letter (FF) in Old English.
G says its hard sound /g/ in most positions.
H is silent in HOUR, HONEST, and HEIR.
J was the last letter added to the English alphabet.
K is silent before N as in KNEE and KNOW.
L is sometimes silent as in WALK, TALK, and CALM.
M is one of the first sounds babies learn to make.
N is the 6th most common letter in English.
P is silent in PSALM, PNEUMONIA, and PSYCHOLOGY.
Q almost ALWAYS needs U — they are best friends.
R is the 'bossy' letter — it changes every vowel it follows.
S is the most common letter used to make plurals.
T is the second most common consonant in English.
English words never end in just V — they add a silent E.
W is silent before R as in WRITE and WRONG.
X at the end of a word says /ks/ — it is a two-sound letter.
Z is the least used letter in English.
SH makes a 'be quiet' sound — shhh!
Voiced TH vibrates your vocal cords — put your hand on your throat to feel it.
CH is the most common sound of this team — like a train: choo-choo!
WH used to be pronounced with a puff of air — some dialects still do.
PH comes from Greek — it replaced the Greek letter Phi.
CK is used right after a short vowel at the end of a syllable.
NK says /ngk/ — the N changes to the /ng/ sound before K.
NG makes a single nasal sound — try saying it without the G!
GH says /g/ at the beginning of words like GHOST.
The K in KN used to be pronounced in Old English!
The W in WR was pronounced 1000 years ago in Old English.
GN words often come from Old English or Latin.
DGE is used right after a short vowel to spell the /j/ sound.
TCH is used right after a short vowel to spell the /ch/ sound.
EE almost always says its long sound — very reliable!
EA saying /ee/ is the most common of its three sounds.
OA almost always says long O — it is very dependable.
OI is used in the middle of words — OY goes at the end.
OY is the end-of-word partner of OI — same sound, different position.
OU is one of the trickiest vowel teams with multiple sounds.
OW says /ow/ in words like COW, HOW, and NOW.
AU comes from Latin words — it always says /aw/.
AW is the end-of-word partner of AU — same sound.
EW saying /oo/ appears in words like FLEW, GREW, and DREW.
Long OO is the 'moon' sound — open and round.
AI is used in the middle of words — AY goes at the end.
AY is the end-of-word partner of AI — same long A sound.
EI says /ay/ in words like VEIN, REIN, and REINDEER.
EY says /ay/ in common words like THEY, GREY, and OBEY.
IE says /ee/ in the middle of words like FIELD and CHIEF.
UE often appears at the end of words after a consonant.
UI is unpredictable — /oo/ in FRUIT but /i/ in BUILD.
AR makes the 'pirate' sound — ARRR!
ER, IR, and UR all make the same sound — the /er/ triplets.
IR sounds the same as ER and UR — you just have to memorize which to use.
UR is the third member of the /er/ sound triplets.
OR makes a unique sound — R 'bosses' the O into a new sound.
IGH is a 3-letter team where GH is silent — only I speaks.
EIGH uses 4 letters to make one sound — the long A!
OUGH can make SIX different sounds — the trickiest phonogram in English!
AUGH uses 4 letters just to say /aw/ — talk about overkill!
TI says /sh/ at the beginning of a suffix — like in NATION.
CI says /sh/ at the beginning of a suffix — like in SPECIAL.
SI says /sh/ in words like SESSION, TENSION, and PASSION.
SCI says /sh/ in just a few words — quite rare!
ED says /ed/ after T or D — adding a whole new syllable.
Q and U are inseparable — Q never goes anywhere without U.
